video_title: Luis Laboy: Why Every Market Is Now an Emerging Market | Hewlett Foundation
transcript: [music] Luis, I am so excited for this conversation. >> Thanks, Z. I'm very excited to be here. >> With these conversations, we're keen to explore your path to the seat, how you spend your time, and really insights into the life of an institutional LP. To start, tell me a little bit about your upbringing. I was very fortunate to grow up in a big family, right? I was uh the fifth of six kids. So, there were six kids, two parents, one grandmother, and apparently my parents thought that wasn't enough. So, they got a St. Bernard, [laughter] right? And uh and here I grew up in this big house, right, with lots of people and thankfully lots of love, laughs and and a lot of strong opinions which obviously invited a lot of debate and you know and all that played out in our daily dinners together and that was some of my fondest memories come from those dinners, you know, and it was they were always full of lively conversation and you know everything from school and what happened at school and friends to current events and and to politics, right? And you know, and think about it, like I remember as early as being about 6, 7 years old, sitting at what was essentially the big kids table, right? The adult table, and listening to all these conversations. For example, like I remember vividly like when we moved to the States, you know, listening to conversations about the Iran hostage crisis back in the early late 70s, right? And and even the 80 election with Carter and Reagan. And you know, it's it's interesting because I think of myself back then and I think I've always been fairly contrarian by nature. And I think those family conversations really sharpened that, you know, and I and investors may appreciate this, right? I understood early on that if you were going to get oxygen in a room full of people that were more experienced or in this case older than me, that you needed to say something different and value added, right? There's no premium in consens consensus thinking, right? And so I I learned to listen carefully, right? And and to see where my view differed and and I kind of developed that over time. And that's kind of a muscle that I still have today. And and actually, funny enough, the thing that I really learned was that provocative thoughts were were like extra credit. Like that's what really lit up that dinner table in that room, right? So then you really think through the contrarian thinking. >> What were some of your early interests? >> So I was into politics as a kid, you know, and I think it's the result of those dinner conversations and you know, and back then there weren't 100 stations and streaming, right? We watched the news together. And and what I realized actually over time and looking back on it, it's not really politics that I liked. I really enjoyed elections. I joined more so than the candidates. I really enjoyed the campaign strategy. You know, it really came down to right, you had a certain amount of money and a certain amount of time before an election and a message. It's like how do you marshall those forces around? And and I think what helped was that at that age and and maybe I was into politics at maybe an unnaturally young age. I was like 13 or so. But you know I think what helped is when we lived in Chicago that I I had a friend that was a kindred spirit, someone that I still talk to today 40 years later. And you know we used to talk a lot about politics together and we studied elections and and in fact there was this one game while other kids were playing video games. We were playing an election simulation game and we actually were studying electoral maps from 1960 to 1992 more or less at that time and you know you really learned a lot about the evolution of of politics and the political landscape which really came in handy as I as I started my career >> when did the interest in finance and investing kick in >> college I started mechanical engineering and that was really following my father's path right go someone once told me I was great at math and science so you become becoming a mechanical engineer. And in the end, I would probably do what he did, which is go into business. And the plan was working fine until my junior year. And it's said junior year that I took my first class in economics. And everything changed. You know, I felt like Neo in the matrix, right? I could see all of the ones and zeros, right? And the professor didn't hurt. He was really inspiring. You know, he would sit at the edge of that platform in class and the vein throbbing at his neck as he was explaining these concepts. and he would lean so far out into the class that I I don't understand how he didn't fall off the platform. And as you can imagine from that, it didn't take very long. I switched to econ. And now I was following my brother's path, my brother Carlos. And and really it was, you know, econ into finance and sellside. The goal was to be an analyst. And that's when I really started to think about finance. But you know what it's really interesting because what I realized is that how much mechanical engineering and econ had in common right it was about studying systems it was about studying resource management right it's it's almost what appealed to me about politics too and what what's interesting is you know we we spend a lot of time of allocators asking managers about their origin stories and often the origin story has to do with you know I love stocks right something to do with Warren Buffett right and they were reading financial statements at a very young age. But for me, my interest in finance came from the top down. I feel like those stocks are from the bottom up. For me, it was the politics. It was the economics. And the one place where that made sense, where those were very important drivers of return were in emerging markets. And so that's where I decided to launch my career. >> How did you get into the financial industry after college? >> I spent a a fair amount of time interviewing because I wanted to start my career in Mexico. I was dead set to start in emerging markets. And you know, quite frankly, I think that's again the resume that I had up to that point, right? The experience I had at that point would fit in well there. And so my first job was actually at Dresnner Bank, Mexico. And you know, uh, we were building out a research team and my job was to report to do one of the dailies. Every research group had a daily and write about the political macro market events of the day. And at Dresnner, I was very fortunate to have as a first boss Martin Benson. He was just terrific and I really credit him for really helping me launch my career. And uh you know two years into my time at Dresnner. So I was doing this daily reporting. I walked into his office and I told him I needed to move to the states. I just wanted to move back and he gave me a piece of advice that I still think about today and it's a piece of advice I give people all the time. He told me I needed a story. And my story was that I I that I couldn't leave yet. I just hadn't completed the arc to be ready for my next job. And so my story is I wanted to be an analyst. And so I immediately taught myself accounting. I started to work on financial modeling. And then while I'm doing all of this, that's where I had an opportunity to meet Anna Marshall for the first time. And uh she was traveling to Mexico with uh to visit companies and she was traveling with Bear Sterns. My brother was working at Bear Sterns at the time and I got a chance to join in with them and that was the first time that I had really truly seen the whole puzzle come together of that top down and the bottom up and how it comes together to lead to investment conclusions and it was mesmerizing. Clearly, I had found my place, right? And so I went off, you know, after the after the visit, I called Anna and I asked her, "What do I need to do if I ever want to work with somebody like you?" And Anna's advice was, "Get your CFA." And I'll tell you, anybody who knows Anna, 25 years later, that would still be her [laughter] same advice to anybody starting today. And so I studied for the CFA. And when I passed level one, I called her up to give her the update. And as luck would have it, she was hiring. And she invited me up to San Francisco. And I about a month later, I was moving to San Francisco to go work with her at Dresnner RCM. That was my first tour of duty with her. I worked there for four years before uh I ended up moving to Everest Capital. And at Everest, I spent about 15 years. There uh I covered Latin America. I was working in emerging markets, Turkey, parts of Europe, Africa. And what was great about Everest is I invested across all asset classes. So now was really broadening my scope, right? We were in equities, debt, commodities, currencies often simultaneously. And it really played again to those strengths, right? The top down, the finding the thematics, the macro, and then obviously marrying that with the with the bottom up. And you know what really stands out from all that time at Everest was actually how it started, right? It wasn't a it wasn't a linear move upward to say the least, right? You know, 6 months in, it's time for reviews and Marco calls me into his office and basically tells me that I'm probably not going to have another review unless my performance improves. How many years into being at Everest were you? >> I was 6 months. So, I clearly felt I was over my head. But, you know, thankfully, even in that moment where I'm doing the internal calculations that I think I'm going to get fired, you know, I had the wherewithal to ask him what he thought I was doing wrong. He saw me work every day. And so, I asked him, you know, what what he suggested and he gave me a piece of advice that I still think about today, right? He goes, "You're trying to be right rather than make money. And our job is to make money." And, you know, I didn't quite know what that looked like. So I asked him to explain and we probably spent about 20 to 30 minutes on that and uh you know if I can visualize it I can do it and so he really helped me out and at the end of the call I stood up and you know trying to you know act confident I said Marco I can do that and he wasn't having some of this fake bravado and he immediately just said I hope so [laughter] which wasn't very uh >> confidence building. You're six months in. You have a heart-to-he heart with your boss. You think you might be getting fired. And then you proceed to spend the next 15 years of your career at Everest. What changed? And what were the various hats that you wore in those 15 years? >> Eight months passed by and Lula had just been elected president. It's early 2000s. Everybody thought that Brazil was going to devalue and default. And I had this the great benefit of doing this spectacular trip coordinated by Emmy Shia. Like I really owe her so much. And we went and we met with the head of the central bank, of the Senate, the head of the House of Reperatives. And then we met with the chief of staff of Lula in his office. And I came back from that trip just believing that the market was wrong, right? That contrarian person, right? Always looking for the opposite. Came in. And I came in, I said, "We need to own Brazil." And so we maxed out our limit in Brazil and that just wasn't enough for Marco. So we went to go put on an options trade and it was a it was a structure that the market had to go up 20% in two months in two months for us to make a 5 to one payout on our premium for the option structure. And you know I still remember Marco holding his finger over the enter button before sending the trade. And he even looked at me and he said are you sure? Of course, I wasn't sure, right? [laughter] But but what I was certain about was that if I said no, I was likely going to get fired in four [laughter] months before my next review. So, I clearly understood the incentive structure that was on the table. And so, I said absolutely, Marco. And you know, wouldn't you know it, within two months, the market up went up 23%. We made our 5 to1 payout on our option strategy, all the money on the cash trade. And by the time of my next review, they had promoted me. They promoted me to partner. And I actually went from almost fired to partner in 12 months. >> I love it. 15 years at an asset manager is a long time. Tell us about some of the various hats that you wore in the 15 years that you were at Everest. >> In addition to investing, I was part of a of a team where I was an analyst and a portfolio manager. So, I was the decision maker as well. And you know, one of the things that I did while I was at Everest is I marketed a lot. And you know, one of the things that I learned, I learned this from Anna early on is, you know, figure out how to add value on the team. And why? Because invariably managers have bad years of performance. And so I figured if I added value that one year that came that was going to be the year that tested me, that I had I had a buffer. I had some other way that I was adding to the team. And so I market a lot and I'm an external thinker, right? So marketing kind of served two purposes, right? It had the defense and the offense, right? The defense was protecting myself against the down air. The offense was that I actually thought a lot about our thesis. Like I thought a lot about the portfolio and and and how I was investing it in those meetings. Like I'm I'm not sure if managers if sorry if LPs at the time really always understood that I was working through ideas >> while uh while we were in our meetings. The other thing was that uh you know I worked on the EM team. I helped launch the frontier markets product and you know by the time that I was leaving Everest I had just studied uh technical analysis to become a chartered market technician and so I was introducing that as well into our process and uh and working on the frontier markets team. What prompted the move from a direct investment seat to an institutional allocator? >> You know, it's funny. The first time that I got exposure to the endowment and foundation world, I was sitting in a meeting with our salesperson and I was listening to the LP talk about how the CIO wanted him in a seat for 10 years. And I'm thinking like, what world do we live in where someone can see for 10 years out, right? I worked at a hedge fund where you know I I was thinking in terms of yeartoyear and I slowly started to learn more about the world and obviously because Anna was there and Anna and I all this time over those 15 years stayed in touch and I got to know more and more about what she did and how they did it and I it became more and more intriguing. I felt what what really happened I think was at the at Everest, you know, our our time horizon was very short, right? And I was just kind of longing for to expand that time horizon to broaden out and I wanted to broaden out out of emerging markets and Latin America. And it just felt like a natural step because it just kind of felt the natural evolution of what I wanted to do, especially after I studied the uh technical analysis. and such fun serendipity to have those points of connectivity with Anna early in your career and then in the latest transition from Everest to Huelet. What was that actual transition like? How did you join the team at HLET? >> On the personal side, I had just finished remodeling a house after torturing myself for a year and a half. And when I say I literally had just thrown out the last box, my girlfriend and I, now my wife, were hosting my parents and my oldest daughter. And Saturday afternoon, I get a call from Anna in the middle of a barbecue and we talked for a while and I hang up and I turn to everybody and I say, "Well, looks like we need to move." And we had lived the place less than a month. So, I went to San Francisco and we I interviewed with the team, got to meet everybody, which is really great because they were they were very helpful in just the ramping up and understanding what was ahead. And within a couple months, I was in San Francisco again working with Anna at Heillet. >> Amazing. How did your time at a hedge fund inform how you approached your new job as an LP? >> There's a lot here, right? I think I think first, it's not just the time at the hedge fund that was helpful. It's the amount of time that I got to spend at Everest that was really helpful because there's obviously the experience of the different asset classes learning how to invest. You know, at Everest, what I really learned was how to make a decision, but I think it's the time that I got to spend at Everest where I really learned kind of the soft parts of an investment firm. You know, let me explain, right? So I spent 15 years with the same team and over those 15 years I actually got to see the arc of people's careers. I got to see the arc of strategies right initiate and close down and I got to see the arc of the whole firm and it was a gift to be in that one place because it let you study. It's almost like with a family, right? It's almost like watching a movie over and over and every time you see the movie you pick up something different, right? And so and that's what it's like to work with the same people for a long time, right? I got to understand the nuances of culture of an investment team. You know, how people's willingness to t take risk changes over time, how my own willingness to take risk changed over time. And and then when Everest closed and the whole team moved to RWC, I got to see how the same group of people, how their energy changed, how a culture changed. And there was a lot of learnings because in the end as allocators we're sitting on the outside trying to understand what's happening on the inside and we're we're left to fill in a lot of gaps. And that experience of those 15 years gave me a lot of material to not necessarily understand and fill in every gap properly but to know which questions to ask and kind of where the trip wires are inside a firm. How did your experience marketing at Everest inform how you wanted to approach manager selection once you joined an LP? >> When you market a lot, a couple things happen, right? There's learnings that come from, you know, there were LPs that challenged me that made me a better investor, you know, and and as an as I was becoming an LP, I wanted to become one of those, right? I wanted to be helpful to managers but you know the the move over the marketing was helpful but there's there's like this little nuance between the two jobs right and there's a difference in cadence in decision making and even in the amount of research and analysis you do for every decision [snorts] and you know you're calibrating that all the time and I had perfected that for a high turnover world of direct investing and when you move over to the to the LP side you make many less decisions and you definitely go much deeper in terms of analysis. So you have to reccalibrate that carefully. You can fall into the trap of making uninformed decisions or falling into analysis paralysis. So that that took a while and the marketing experience was actually really helpful in that because as I started manager selection, you know, it it really helped me to understand how to ask managers questions, right? It really helped me to kind of dig through the presentation. In fact, I I never even wanted to pick up the presentation, right? I wanted to talk I I understood I think better. I think it allowed me to get into the perceptual position of the manager more organically and not ask questions trying to understand what they do from an outside perspective but actually how it feels to be in the seat and sit on the other side of that table. >> What were some examples of questions that you feel you took from that experience? >> You know, I'll tell you there's a great example when I first started uh at Everest. we weren't necessarily known um for the most stringent riskmanagement practices and uh and I did a meeting with a manager I took a meeting with a manager early on and I listened I asked them about risk management I noticed they hadn't talked about it the whole meeting and I asked them about kind of how they manage risk and I got this very long answer with lots of words and I realized that's kind of the answer I used to give so I actually stopped them and I said look I I don't think you realize like I just spent 15 years marketing and I used to give your exact answer. What I'm picking up here is that you guys really don't have a very strong risk management practice [laughter] and he laughed and said you're right. Right. So it actually let me cut through. Right. Again I understood how to interpret what they were saying and put it into into a question. So I think it really just helped me break through and understand and ask questions I think with a layer of empathy, right? with an understanding of what are the different pressure points that they're looking at because I I think the goal is to ask managers questions that hopefully create new thoughts, but you never want to come across like you're secondguessing a manager, right? That's not our job. Our job is to understand them and to understand whether our strategies are aligned. And so really a lot of my questions I think are I think come from that perspective and I hope that you know that's the way it comes across. How did you initially spend your time when you first joined Hulet? >> I think at first just questioning why I had made the change but uh because I felt like I was drinking from a fire hose for years. I had the great benefit of uh of stepping into just a spectacular team and and and really the benefit that the portfolio the public e public equity portfolio was mature and just and full of just really great managers. Right. This is a portfolio that was actually put together by Anna herself and I was taking that over. So there was a great benefit to that because on the portfolio construction side which is where I spent most of my time at the beginning because we already had this this built out manager set and what we were actually doing at the beginning was we were concentrating the portfolio. And so I really spent most of the time on the portfolio construction side but what was really helpful is I understood Anna's philosophy of portfolio construction. She she was my mentor. That's that's how I even learned how to build portfolios on the direct side. The only difference is I was learning context. I was learning what does it mean when it's managers instead of stocks and the different decision-m that we have to make. And so in that portfolio construction, what I really learned in those first years is just understanding the philosophy, understanding how the different strategies came together and you know and and that was important. Having that ability was important because that really helped inform the manager selection piece >> in those early years when you're spending time on the portfolio construction side. What's an example of your role in a real decision that was made? A good example of that is uh is during COVID, right? And CO, you know, by the time COVID came around, I had been doing this a few years. And CO was the first time that I actually had a moment to step back and I got to just you're alone in your office in your house, right, for months. And so I really started to do deep dives into the portfolio, you know, and one of the things that we really started to focus on then was that we I we felt there was a transition happening in the market. We thought that there's a transition happening in economies. And you know what we did is we just I started to break down the portfolio in ways that really we ended up starting to uh reduce our exposure to for example value managers. We thought value would just be a tougher place to be going forward. And so it's that breaking down of the portfolio, presenting that to Anna. I started making these presentations, these 10, 15 page presentations to entertain myself as I broke down the portfolio. And you know, I think that really helped us in understanding where the structure was today, where we thought it was going. We had extensive conversations about that and some of the decisions we needed to make. And there it was reducing, like I said, some of the value managers that we had in the book. and then transitioning into a more quality kind of compounding portfolio that was becoming more prevalent at the time. >> As you've progressed in your tenure at Huelet, how has your allocation of responsibilities and how you balance your time changed? The biggest change has been that right at the beginning we had a larger manager roster and we were concentrating that manager roster and and this is where the the buy side experience came in right that we didn't just look at it as managers we also looked at the underlying companies we started to you know I started to build out spreadsheets that showed all the underlying positions that we had to and we really thought about as a portfolio of companies and you know so as we started to get that core portfolio IO down to a certain size, then the shift really starts to become over to manager selection because once you have a core portfolio, eventually over the course of 10 years that I've been at at Huelet, well, eventually you have life cycles of managers and eventually you have to start searching for new managers. So, I went from what was a very large mature roster. We concentrated it and now we needed to go find the next generation of manager. And so what ended up happening is, you know, now I probably spend half and half, right, of my time really on that portfolio construction, which is so important, getting the sizing right, of of managers and sizing risk correctly. But then it's also the flip side of now sourcing those managers. >> How much time goes into managers who aren't currently in your portfolio versus managers who are? >> An increasing amount of time. It's something there's a tension there all the time, right? Because we give as much time to new managers, we want to give as much time to new managers as we do to existing roster, right? Because getting what you have right is as important as getting finding finding that next manager. And that's always a tension in the portfolio. And that was even in the structure of what we were doing, right? Like we learned early on the mix of explore versus exploit, right? So exploit means, right? We have this set of managers and we want to maximize the return of those and that's the portfolio construction side. But at the same time you constantly need a new a certain velocity of new ideas into the book. So one of the structures that was changed over time was instead of just having one portfolio what we basically broke it down was we had one core portfolio because we want to maintain that discipline of having a limited set of managers and then making room for new relationships. And so as that structure changed and we started to increase the amount of exploit the velocity of new ideas then obviously that takes a lot of time and and it takes a lot of time to meet managers and it takes a lot of time to to triage and to to really get to know them. >> How does a new manager make its way into such a concentrated book? >> That was the rub for a while, right? That when you have a concentrated roster of really spectacular managers, like we're really just we're very grateful that we have access to these really great managers. the hurdle becomes very high. And so one of the structures that I did is I like I said I kind of broke the portfolio into two where I had this core portfolio and then you carve out a piece of the portfolio for this this next generation set of managers where the hurdle isn't lower. We're still trying to find the same kind of quality or the high level manager. The difference is that we accept in fact if you go back to my equation of like make money don't be right rather than be right. Right? Those calibrate differently for the core portfolio than from the new manager set. You're giving yourself room to be more wrong there because so you're allowing a little bit more churn. And so what we did is once we changed that structure, we gave ourselves room with what is kind of a next generation portion of the book then we were able to like lower the hurt. It's it's really you're lowering the confidence hurdle. You're not lowering the hurdle of quality. >> What are the characteristics of the managers that you look for in that carved out structure for new managers? Well, we have a whole set of characteristics and and that continues to evolve and and you know, it's really interesting because the reason why so we build out the set of characteristics and the reason why we do that is because one of the most dangerous things that happens is that you look for what you want instead of what you need. So, you know this, I'm a girl dad, right? And I I' as much as I'd like to blame her for this, like we watched reality TV and one of the shows we watched was Married at First Sight, okay? And the structure of the show Married at First Sight is that there's this set of experts that will pick out your mate for you. So instead of you doing it based on your attraction or the participants, it was done by a set of experts. There was a framework. And I never forgot that because invariably these didn't work because people always went back to what they wanted rather than what they needed which is what the experts were picking, right? And so I very much focus on that with with security with manager selection which is if I don't have a set of clear criteria ahead of time, I'm going to end up picking the shiny object that resonates with me, right? And then you're going to be introducing a certain potentially a certain factor risk which is your own your own biases into the portfolio. And so these these criteria the criteria that we're talking about here it's changed over time because it moves with the opportunity set and that's one of the things I think we do well that we're flexible and we're adaptable and we're constantly looking forward at what we need from a manager. So it's it's a set of criteria that that that has shifted over time. >> I feel pretty confident that this is the first time manager selection has been comped to married at first sight on capital allocators. So thanks for that. How do you decide when to move on from a manager? >> It's always such a hard decision, right? And and this is what I was speaking about earlier about you. The cadence of decision-m is so different in what we do. So, one of the things that I've done and we've borrowed this from Annie Duke and her book quit, right, is we've put together what we call as a kill list. So, for every manager in our book, we have what we call kill lists and key debates. And it's basically a set of items that we're always looking at for every managers whether that's new manager or existing managers areas where we're trying to gain confidence where we're trying to understand because we always want to be if we have to redeem for a manager we want it to be for fundamental reasons. We never want to be chasing performance. And usually what ends up happening more times than not, Hank, is that you end up redeeming from a manager because we're shifting our strategy. We're fix we're shifting our perspective on what's changing in the market and we have to redeem from a manager that otherwise I think is extraordinarily talented and I wish them great success but just no longer there's just no longer an alignment between what we're what we need for the portfolio and what they deliver as a manager and their strategy >> who is involved on the team in this entire process around manager selection [clears throat] >> you know for so for the public equity portfolio an and I are the ones spearhead that and but the reality is that we're we're one team, one approach. So, you know, I think this is the best part of our Monday morning meetings and it almost reminds me of dinner back when I was a kid, right? Where, you know, we're meeting on Mondays and and I love to do this too, just like Tom, one of the directors there does, which is share halfbaked ideas, share how our conviction or our confidence in a manager is changing and really put it out there for the team. So even though Anna and I are are the ones that are making that final decision on a manager really we try and get the input of the whole team and so any decision that we make never comes as a surprise to every anybody and potentially has the input of everybody. We're a small team. It's myself, Brett Johnson, Tom Mitchkowski as directors uh and Anna as the CIO. And you know, the reality is that Anna plays a very pivotal role across all of our asset classes. So, we have this natural sounding board with Anna. She's deeply involved with all of us. And so, it's it's interesting. I just don't like I I get so much from Anna and that experience and those conversations and we meet the managers together that I really don't I don't miss having a direct report to to to bounce ideas with and to do that work with. >> I'd love to tap into that. uh direct rapport you have with Anna. How much of that is informed by your previous working relationship and having known her for 30 years? >> You know, I remember the it was like the second or the third day I started at the foundation and I was sitting at my desk and I heard her voice in the background and it was just the oddest feeling. I felt like I was 27 again, right? Like it just felt like no time had passed. And uh look, I think without without a doubt, you know, one of the great things of working with Anna is that we see eye to eye on the basic structure and philosophy of things. And it's not that we agree with each other. There's no consensus thinking. There's a natural, you know, one of the things Anna really prizes is innovation and evolution of thought and creative thinking. And I'm definitely contrarian by nature, but we start, we have that same base. So we challenge and question each other and we really riff really well and brainstorm. So, you know, that that early time is really great because it sets the base, but I think what's really, and this is why I wanted to work with her again, like Anna leaves a lot of room for growth, a lot of room for you to run and to think, and I think that's what works so well in the relationship, and that's what that's part of the culture that she sets across the team as well. What are some of the other philosophical commonalities that you and her share from an investment perspective? >> Oh gosh. Um, we have a very uh a very similar view on just riskreward frameworks of managers. You know, we again we were a manager together, right? So we like I said we know how to sh we share the philosophy of building a portfolio. We share the philosophy of manager selection. And in fact, that criteria that, you know, we've put together over time is a shared criteria that we've talked about.
